chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, called from ASN.1→X509 conversion chain. This is app/SDK crypto parsing code, not allocator/sanitizer; likely a logic or memory bug in parsing malformed ASN.1 structures. Fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input stands in for realistic inputs (cert blobs) that in production come from attacker-influenced sources (smart card, network, files). ASN.1 and X.509 parsing is historically fragile and often vulnerable to length/child-count miscalculations leading to out-of-bounds access or infinite recursion; the function name suggests computing child count of an ASN.1 SEQUENCE/SET. However, we do not have the decompiled implementation or exact termination reason (e.g., SIGSEGV vs. abort), only that process termination occurs in this function. Without code, we can’t prove whether it’s a deliberate bounds-check failure (benign abort) or an unintended over-read/write. Still, the fact the fuzzer caused process termination in the core parsing routine, away from harness-specific bridges, and the function is part of a certificate load path that takes external cert data, makes a real parsing bug quite plausible. No evidence points to the harness misusing JNI (Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo is a normal bridge and lib map is consistent). On balance, classify as likely vulnerability: ASN.1 child-count computation bug reachable from attacker-controlled certificate data, with at least DoS impact and plausible memory corruption, but details unknown. Confidence moderate (0.7) due to missing binary/JNI/Java code and unknown exact crash mode.

Given the missing low-level evidence, cannot assign a precise CWE beyond generic parsing/memory issues; safest is to treat as potential out-of-bounds / improper validation in ASN.1 parsing with DoS potential. Severity medium: definitely an application crash on malformed cert, but RCE not proven.
reasons: Crash occurs in core ASN.1 child-count computation (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) during X.509 certificate parsing, not in harness glue or allocator.
Function is reached through CERT_Load and a JNI method jGetCertInfo, which in production will process attacker-influenced certificate blobs (e.g., smart card or remote certs).

Process termination in such parsing logic under fuzzing commonly indicates missing bounds checks or incorrect length/child calculations that can cause out-of-bounds access or at least uncontrolled abort, representing a DoS and potentially more serious memory issues.
No signs that the crash is caused solely by unrealistic JNI misuse; call stack and library map match a normal app-native path.
recommendations: Obtain and review the implementation of Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 parsing functions to identify and fix any missing bounds checks or incorrect length/child calculations.
Add strict validation of ASN.1 lengths, nesting depth, and child counts before iterating or indexing into buffers; ensure all loops are bounded by actual buffer sizes.
Enable and test with sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) on libUbikeyJni.so using fuzzed and adversarial certificates to confirm the exact failure mode and rule out further memory-corruption paths.
assumptions: jGetCertInfo processes certificate data that is at least partially attacker-controlled (e.g., smart card, network, or file input).
The fuzzer approximates realistic malformed cert inputs and does not violate invariants that production Java code would always uphold.
Process termination is due to an internal parsing error (e.g., OOB, assertion) rather than an injected fault from the harness.
limitations: No direct decompiled view of Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum or related functions, so specific bug pattern (OOB read, write, or abort) is unknown.
Exact signal/exception type at crash (SIGSEGV, SIGABRT, etc.) is not provided, limiting precision in classifying the vulnerability.
Java-level call graph for jGetCertInfo is not available, so exact attacker-control degree of certificate input is inferred, not proven.

exploit.trigger_method: Malicious or malformed X.509 certificate processed via UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process a crafted certificate via the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo (e.g., by supplying a smart card, token, or remote cert).
libUbikeyJni.so is built without full memory-safety mitigations that would entirely prevent abuse of parsing bugs beyond DoS.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1-encoded X.509 certificate with malformed child element counts or inconsistent length fields targeting the Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum logic.
Deliver this certificate through whatever mechanism the app uses to obtain certs (smart card, file import, or network-provided cert) so that Java calls UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo.
When CERT_Load parses the certificate, it triggers Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and then Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, hitting the bug and causing process termination or memory corruption.
Iterate on the malformed ASN.1 structure under a debugger or sanitizer build to explore whether the bug can be shaped into more than a denial of service (e.g., controlled out-of-bounds write).
exploit.notes: PoC paths and activity names are placeholders; actual exploit requires identifying the app activity or component that imports or inspects certificates and ensuring it uses UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo. This is a certificate/ASN.1 parsing pipeline inside libUbikeyJni.so.

Java side: UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo(byte[] bArr) directly passes an unbounded byte array to the native code. No length/structure validation in Java. Fuzz harness fuzz_one_input presumably maps the fuzz buffer to this byte[] and calls jGetCertInfo, so the DER/ASN.1 data is fully attacker-controlled.

Crash reason: __memcpy termination strongly suggests out-of-bounds memory access or invalid pointer/size used during a memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or deeper in the ASN.1 decoding path. ASN.1/DER parsers classically copy variable-length fields from the encoded buffer into internal structures; if there is a bug in length calculation or boundary checks, memcpy can read/write past buffer limits, leading to a crash.

Even though I cannot decompile the specific functions due to tool errors, the call pattern and function names are consistent with custom ASN.1 parsing and X.509 certificate conversion logic, not harness artifacts. There is no indication that fuzzing passed impossible parameters (e.g., NULL env or JNI pointers); only the certificate bytes are attacker-controlled.

Given that: (1) memcpy is crashing; (2) path is in ASN.1 → X509 conversion, (3) input is directly attacker-controlled bytes, and (4) such code frequently has parsing bugs leading to OOB, this is highly likely a genuine memory safety vulnerability, not just a benign abort. The bug is reachable in real app usage whenever untrusted certificate blobs are parsed.

Impact: Out-of-bounds memcpy can cause heap or stack corruption, potential code execution or at least denial-of-service. In the context of a banking app’s cryptographic JNI, malformed certificates from a remote server or MITM could trigger this. Precise exploitability (RCE vs DoS) cannot be confirmed without the exact memcpy arguments and allocator behavior, but severity is at least high.
CWE mapping: generic buffer overflow / out-of-bounds write during memory copy (CWE-787, CWE-119) or possibly out-of-bounds read if memcpy source overruns buffer (CWE-125).

Exploit outline: an attacker provides a crafted certificate/DER blob to the app (e.g., via network or imported cert file). App calls UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo with that blob; native CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST perform unsafe memcpy and corrupt memory, leading to crash or controlled overwrite depending on heap layout.

Limitations: No direct view of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implementation, no exact memcpy size calculations, no confirmation whether overwriting return addresses is possible. Also, I don’t know the exact Java callgraph from app entrypoints to jGetCertInfo, but presence of jGetCertInfo as a public native in a crypto API strongly suggests reachability from app code based on incoming data.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy inside ASN.1/X.509 parsing functions of libUbikeyJni.so, strongly indicating an out-of-bounds memory copy.
Java UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo(byte[]) passes attacker-controlled certificate bytes directly into native parsing without visible validation.
Fuzz harness only replaces the certificate payload; it does not introduce impossible JNI states, so the crash likely reflects a real parser bug.
ASN.1 decoding bugs commonly lead to memory corruption, giving at least reliable denial-of-service and potential code execution.
recommendations: Audit and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, and CERT_Load around all memcpy/memmove calls; ensure lengths are derived from validated buffer bounds and checked before copying.
Add strict DER/ASN.1 length validation (including overflow checks) before any memory allocation or memcpy, or replace custom parsers with a well-tested library.
Introduce runtime protections: compile with stack canaries, ASan in testing, and add input fuzzing for all UbikeyJni certificate-related JNI methods (jGetCertInfo, jGetCert, jCreateVerifyData, etc.).
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo is callable in normal app workflows with certificate data derived from network or external inputs.
The fuzz harness does not pass NULL or otherwise invalid JNI environment/objects beyond what a real app could provide.
The crash in __memcpy corresponds to an out-of-bounds access triggered by crafted ASN.1 lengths, not a deliberate abort inside libUbikeyJni.
limitations: Could not decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and related functions to see exact memcpy arguments and buffer bounds.
Do not know exact allocator behavior or whether the overflow is on source or destination.
Full Java callgraph to jGetCertInfo and its actual use in the app is not available; reachability is inferred from naming and standard usage patterns.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER/X.509 certificate blob that the app passes into UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo(byte[]).
prerequisites: Attacker can influence certificate or key data consumed by the app (e.g., via network connection, imported cert file, or intercepted TLS handshake).
App actually calls UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo on that untrusted blob in some workflow.
No additional input validation is performed before invoking the JNI method.
exploit_pipeline: Prepare a malformed DER-encoded certificate whose ASN.1 length fields cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an incorrect copy size or pointer.
Deliver this certificate to the banking app through a reachable input channel (e.g., server response, imported certificate file, or crafted QR/intent leading to certificate processing).
Cause the Java layer to invoke UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo(byte[]) with the crafted byte array.
In native code, CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST call memcpy with attacker-influenced length, leading to heap/stack corruption and process crash.
With careful heap grooming and repeated attempts, potentially pivot the overflow into a more controlled memory corruption primitive for code execution, depending on platform mitigations.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.<SomeActivity> --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der
# Alternatively, in an instrumented test harness, call UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo directly with the contents of crafted_cert.der
exploit.notes: Exact activity/class used to feed the certificate into jGetCertInfo is unknown; PoC assumes there is an existing workflow that reads a certificate from disk or network and passes it to UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo.

chain_of_thought: Crash in __memcpy indicates a memory access violation during a copy; next frame Decode_DER_ASN1_ST then CERT_Load, then JNI function Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo, so the first app-level frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so.

Tooling cannot currently decompile or locate the symbols by name due to HTTP 404 errors from the Ghidra MCP search_functions_by_name endpoint, so I cannot inspect the exact memcpy call site, its src/dst/len expressions, or do precise backward dataflow in native code. I must reason using the stack plus typical patterns of DER/ASN.1 parsing in certificate libraries.

CERT_Load is likely taking attacker-supplied certificate bytes (from smart card / token / possibly remote input) and passes them to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST for ASN.1 DER decoding. Within such decoders, it is common to have length fields embedded within the ASN.1 structures; unsafe memcpy often arises if those length fields are not thoroughly validated against the actual buffer length, causing out-of-bounds read/write.

The fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input, feeding arbitrary data into the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo. The Java side is not available, but in production that method will typically accept a byte[] containing a certificate or APDU payload. The fuzzer therefore approximates realistic attacker control over the certificate bytes. The memcpy crash implies that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST attempted to copy more bytes than the source or destination buffer supports, or used a bad pointer derived from parsing the fuzzed DER.

Since this is a pure native memcpy crash in an internal DER decoder with no indication of harness-only issues such as intentionally passing NULL pointers or impossible ownership patterns, and the operation clearly depends on decoded length fields coming from untrusted input (certificate bytes), this is strongly indicative of an input-driven memory corruption bug (CWE-787 / CWE-120 style). The fact that we see Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load (not generic libc/allocator) argues the failure is in app code, not the harness.

I cannot see bounds checks or mitigations in the native code due to tool limitations; however, memcpy crashing is itself the concrete symptom of an out-of-bounds access. Given the call chain and typical patterns, the most plausible root cause is insufficient validation of length fields in the DER/ASN.1 decoding logic, which is directly driven by attacker-controlled certificate bytes coming from the JNI bridge, making this a genuine vulnerability.

Exploitation would at least allow denial-of-service via crafted certificates, and potentially memory corruption suitable for code execution, depending on buffer layout and protections. Lacking precise code, I conservatively treat severity as high rather than critical but mark exploitability as theoretical/practical with medium-high confidence.

No Java callgraph entries were provided beyond the single JNI bridge method name, so I cannot fully trace from app entry to JNI, but nothing suggests this path is restricted to trusted-only input. The fuzz harness plausibly imitates app behaviour reading certificate data from external sources (NFC token, smart card, file, or remote server).
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in app library libUbikeyJni.so, indicating out-of-bounds memory access during copy.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load strongly suggest custom ASN.1/DER certificate parsing where length fields from untrusted certificate data control memcpy size or offsets.
Fuzz harness feeds arbitrary input through JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo into certificate loading logic, approximating realistic attacker-controlled certificate bytes.
No evidence that the crash is solely due to harness misuse (e.g., intentionally passing null pointers or impossible states); instead crash arises from data-dependent parsing and memcpy.
recommendations: Manually decompile and audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load implementations, focusing on all memcpy/memmove calls and ensuring lengths are strictly bounded by actual buffer sizes.
Add robust ASN.1/DER length validation (including nested lengths and constructed types) before performing any memory copies; reject inputs with inconsistent or oversized lengths early.
Integrate address sanitizers (ASan/Scudo) and targeted fuzzing on CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST with structured DER corpora to detect further edge-case overflows.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo accepts or constructs certificate/DER data from potentially untrusted sources (cards, files, network).
The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input is a faithful approximation of how attacker-controlled input reaches CERT_Load in the real app.
The memcpy crash implies a genuine out-of-bounds access rather than an artificially injected fault from the fuzz environment.
limitations: Ghidra MCP failed to return decompilation for Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, and the JNI method, so exact variable flows and bounds checks cannot be inspected.
Java source and callgraph beyond the JNI bridge name were not available, so precise attacker-control paths and any high-level validation could not be confirmed.
No register/memory dump at the crash site was provided, so it is unclear whether the overflow is read-only, write, or how large the overrun is.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 blob that is processed via UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process an arbitrary or partially controlled certificate/ASN.1 blob (e.g., via smart card, NFC token, file import, or network response).
The vulnerable library libUbikeyJni.so is present and the JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo is reachable in the deployed app.
Standard Android protections (ASLR, NX, stack canaries) are present, so reliable code execution would require careful heap/stack grooming.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malformed DER/ASN.1 certificate whose internal length fields cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an out-of-bounds memcpy size or pointer.

Deliver this certificate blob to the app via a realistic channel (e.g., importing a certificate file, presenting a smart card/token, or injecting a network response that the app passes to UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo).
Trigger the app functionality that invokes UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo with the attacker-controlled blob, causing CERT_Load to call Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
When __memcpy is invoked with oversized length or invalid pointers, it performs an out-of-bounds memory access, leading to process crash (DoS) and potentially controllable memory corruption.
Iteratively refine the DER payload based on observed crashes and memory layout to attempt control over adjacent memory regions and, if feasible, hijack control flow.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der
exploit.notes: The exact app entrypoint and how certificates are supplied are unknown; PoC commands assume there is an activity that can be triggered with a file path extra to load a certificate. In practice, the researcher should identify the real activity/intent that causes UbikeyJni.jGetCertInfo to be called and adapt the commands accordingly.
